content:
A passing suite is not evidence if the oracle and the implementation share an author.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The pull request landed on a Tuesday with forty-seven files and a green checkmark. Coverage went up. Lint passed. Integration tests passed. The author had used an agent for both the feature and the test suite.
Nobody merged it that day. Not because anyone believed the code was malicious. Because the evidence chain was circular.
The implementation asserted behavior X. The tests asserted behavior X. Both were generated from the same prompt thread, separated by a newline and the word “also write tests.”
That is not verification. That is agreement.
Green CI Is a Claim, Not a Proof
Continuous integration answers one narrow question: did the artifacts we defined as gates succeed on this commit?
It does not answer whether those gates measure the right thing. It does not answer whether the tests would fail if the product were wrong in a way the author never imagined.
When a human writes code and a human writes tests, there is at least a chance of adversarial thinking. Two imperfect minds, two blind spots, some friction.
When one session produces both sides of the contract, the blind spots correlate. The model optimizes for plausibility and closure. Tests that pass are closure. Tests that challenge assumptions are harder to generate because the assumptions were never stated.
I have seen this pattern enough times to treat it as a default risk, not an edge case:
→ Happy-path tests that mirror the implementation line for line → Mocks that return exactly what the production code expects, never what a hostile caller sends → Assertions on string literals copied from the generated error messages → Integration tests that hit a fake in-memory store with none of the failure modes of the real dependency → Coverage numbers inflated by testing private helpers the agent created to make itself feel organized
The suite runs in ninety seconds. It proves the suite runs in ninety seconds.
The Oracle Problem in Agent-Authored Work
In testing theory, the oracle is the source of truth about correct behavior. Without an independent oracle, you cannot distinguish a correct program from a confident one.
Agents collapse the oracle into the implementation unless you force separation.
The fix is not “ban agents from writing tests.” The fix is structural: tests must trace to requirements that existed before generation, and those requirements must be hostile to the first draft.
Concrete practices that work on real teams:
→ Write acceptance criteria in plain language before any code or test generation → Have a second reviewer write or approve the test plan before approving the implementation → Require at least one test derived from a production incident, a support ticket, or a documented edge case the feature prompt never mentioned → Run mutation testing or fault injection on critical paths and refuse merges where mutants survive silently → Compare behavior against a golden trace from staging, not against the agent’s description of behavior
If your test review is “does this look reasonable,” you are reviewing tone, not correctness.
What We Changed After a Near Miss
We had a billing adjustment service. Agent wrote the proration logic. Agent wrote the tests. CI green. Staging green for the demo accounts.
Production had a customer on a leap-year anniversary with a mid-cycle plan change and a credit from a failed charge three weeks prior. The service double-applied the credit. The tests never constructed that timeline because the prompt never mentioned leap years or partial failure recovery.
The bug cost less money than the embarrassment. The retrospective cost more time than the feature.
After that, we split the workflow:

• Human writes the spec and the failure catalog.

• Agent generates implementation against the spec.

• Different human (or different session with the spec pinned, never the implementation pasted in) generates tests against the spec only.

• CI must include at least one test tagged oracle-external that a reviewer can trace to a non-generated source.
We also added a merge gate: if test files and implementation files share the same generation transcript ID in the PR description, a bot comments and blocks until someone attests independent test design.
Draconian? Maybe. Cheaper than finance sending a screenshot.
Coverage Metrics Make the Problem Invisible
Coverage rewards existence of execution, not quality of assertion.
An agent can achieve eighty-five percent coverage by testing getters, testing its own DTO mappers, and asserting that a function “does not throw” when fed its own factory output.
Dashboards love the number. Managers love the trend line. The number becomes a substitute for the question “would we know if this were wrong?”
Better signals:
→ Defect escape rate per module after agent-heavy changes → Ratio of tests that fail on first run against a seeded buggy implementation → Time-to-diagnose when someone deliberately breaks an invariant in a game-day exercise → Reviewer checklist completion on independent oracles, not line counts
Green CI plus rising coverage plus rising incidents is a familiar chart. It means your gates measure agreement, not alignment with reality.
How to Review Agent-Written Tests Without Reading Every Line
You cannot read every assertion in a forty-file PR. You can sample for independence.
Ask these questions on the test diff:
→ Could these tests pass if the implementation returned hard-coded constants? → Is any test naming a behavior that appears nowhere in the pre-generation spec? → Do error cases use messages that only exist because the implementation invented them? → Are boundary values chosen with arithmetic, or copied from the code under test? → Would a staff engineer who did not write either side recognize the scenarios as product risks?
If the answer pattern is wrong, stop. Request regeneration from the spec with the implementation redacted from context.
Treat “tests added” in a PR description as neutral information, like “comments added.” The default posture for agent-authored test suites should be skepticism until proven independent.
Staging Can Lie the Same Way CI Does
Staging green is not independence either if staging tests were generated in the same session. We once shipped a payment retry feature where staging passed because the staging webhook simulator was also agent-authored and shared the same incorrect assumption about idempotency keys.
The fix was boring: one manual curl against production-like config, one recorded trace from a real sandbox vendor, one test that replayed the vendor’s actual error payload from last quarter’s incident. Those three checks caught what four hundred generated assertions missed.
What Good Independence Looks Like in a PR
Good independence is boring in the diff. Tests reference ticket numbers. Scenarios name customer segments from the spec. Assertions use values computed in the test file, not imported constants from production helpers.
When I see a test file that reads like a mirror of the service file with _test suffix energy, I do not argue line by line. I send one comment: regenerate from spec with implementation redacted. The second submission tells me everything about whether the author understands the job.
The Cultural Shift Nobody Schedules
Teams want velocity. Independence slows the first merge. It speeds everything after.
The engineers who feel fastest with agents often resist separate test authorship because it breaks the flow of one prompt, one result, one green bar. That flow is exactly the hazard.
Leadership can help by stopping praise for coverage percentage and starting praise for caught mismatches before production. Celebrate the test that failed on first run because it encoded a requirement the implementation ignored. That failure is the system working.
Your CI is a clerk. It records what you told it to record. If you tell it to record self-consistency, it will do that job perfectly and lie to you with a green icon.
Separate the oracle from the author. Pin requirements before generation. Review tests as strategy, not syntax. Treat a passing suite as one input to a judgment, not the end of judgment.
When you want the production instincts that make independent oracles easier to write — failure modes, observability patterns, the difference between liveness and correctness under load — The Production Engineering Library: The Complete Collection for Production Systems, System Design & Backend Engineering is the reference shelf I keep next to the spec template.
For scenario practice that trains you to ask what would break before the agent agrees with itself, Froquiz has 10,000+ questions across SQL, Docker, Git, AWS, Java, Python, JavaScript, React, microservices, and Senior Dev Challenge scenarios that feel like real review pressure, not flashcards.
